source_file: Original draft for FlutterCon USA 2026 roundtable
question: Q6: Practical Next Steps

sections.prompt: Who here has a specific thing they want to learn or build in the next few months?
sections.hint: Examples: Flutter fundamentals, native mobile, state management, testing, backend integration, AI tooling, product design, deployment, and verification habits.
no_hands: What makes choosing the next step difficult?
How do you deal with the sheer number of competing skills you could be learning?
What would help you pick one focus for the next month?
hands_up: What is your next learning priority and why?
How does AI change the way you plan to learn it?
What will you build, ship, or verify to know you improved?
What would you stop learning for now?
follow_ups: What is a realistic 30-day Flutter learning goal?
How do you avoid collecting courses instead of building?
What would make your learning visible to employers or collaborators?

sections.prompt: Has a side project or portfolio piece ever directly led to an opportunity for you?
sections.hint: Examples: multi-platform apps, AI-powered tools, local business apps, internal dashboards, accessibility-focused products, monetized apps, and products with real users.
no_hands: What makes project choice less important than fundamentals?
Which project ideas feel overdone or weak now?
How can beginners choose a project that is achievable?
hands_up: What kind of Flutter project would genuinely impress you in 2026?
How should AI be used in the project without becoming the whole story?
What project proves cross-platform value clearly?
How do you scope a project so it ships?
follow_ups: What is one project idea you would recommend to a student?
What is one project idea you would recommend to a working developer?
How do you think about who a portfolio project should serve first?

sections.prompt: Has anyone started asking different questions before joining a company because of AI?
sections.hint: Examples: team maturity, product domain, platform requirements, native complexity, AI expectations, testing culture, salary, mentorship, and whether AI investment is paired with hiring or cuts.
no_hands: What makes Flutter opportunities hard to evaluate?
Which questions would you ask before joining a Flutter team?
How do you avoid choosing only based on technology?
hands_up: What signals make a Flutter opportunity attractive?
What red flags would make you hesitate: no mentorship, vague AI strategy, rushed rewrites, or shrinking teams?
How important is the product domain compared with the framework?
How does a team's AI adoption affect your interest in joining them?
follow_ups: What interview question reveals a healthy Flutter team?
How do you judge whether a company uses AI to augment developers or avoid developing people?
What opportunity would you take even if Flutter were not the main stack?

sections.prompt: Who's been in the Flutter ecosystem for more than a couple of years?
sections.hint: Examples: learn fundamentals earlier, ship more, add native/backend depth, use AI deliberately, build product sense, or avoid chasing every tool.
no_hands: What advice would stay exactly the same?
Which old advice still works surprisingly well?
hands_up: What would you tell your past self to learn sooner?
What would you tell your past self to stop worrying about?
How would Flutter fit into that advice?
What habit would have compounded the most over time?
follow_ups: What advice sounds smart but is actually harmful now?
What is one small action people can take after this roundtable?
What should the Flutter community help newcomers understand better?

wrap_up: After this discussion, how has your thinking about Flutter as a career bet changed?
What is one concrete next step you will take in the next 30 days?
What should this community do to help people navigate AI-driven career uncertainty?
